MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Feb-18-2022 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Kirsten Fish, Esq. (408.261.4226; kfish@nkf-law.com) of Needham Kepner & Fish LLP for Plaintiff.
Douglas Thompson, Esq. (310.576.2106; Douglas.Thompson@bclplaw.com) of Bryan Cave Leighton Paiser LLP for Defendants, B of A, et al.
Alicia Ginsberg, Esq. (415.477.5700; alicia.ginsberg@troutman.com) of Troutman Pepper Hamilton Sanders LLP for Defendants, Wells Fargo, et al.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Defendants Bank of America, N.A., Bank of America Corporation, and Nancy Becerra's (collectively "Defendants") motion to strike portions Plaintiff Chan-Hie Kim's Complaint related to punitive damages is denied.

Defendants argue that Plaintiff fails to state sufficient specific facts to support an award of punitive damages, and that Plaintiff fails to meet the employer liability bar pursuant to Civil Code sec 3294(b). Civil Code section 3294(b) provides: "An employer shall not be liable for damages pursuant to subdivision (a), based upon acts of an employee of the employer, unless the employer had advance knowledge of the unfitness of the employee and employed him or her with a conscious disregard of the rights or safety of others or authorized or ratified the wrongful conduct for which the damages are awarded or was personally guilty of oppression, fraud, or malice. With respect to a corporate employer, the advance knowledge and conscious disregard, authorization, ratification or act of oppression, fraud, or malice must be on the part of an officer, director, or managing agent of the corporation." "In determining whether a complaint states facts sufficient to sustain punitive damages, the challenged allegations must be read in context with the other facts alleged in the complaint. Further, even though certain language pleads ultimate facts or conclusions of law, such language when read in context with the facts alleged as to defendants' conduct may adequately plead the evil motive requisite to recovery of punitive damages." (Monge v. Superior Court (1986) 176 Cal. App. 3d 503, 510.)

Here, the complaint alleges Plaintiff, an 85-year-old customer, "walked into his long-time [BANA/BAC] branch and met in person, face-to-face with a teller, who then would call over a bank manager to approve his wire transfers" with "no questions asked by [Defendants]." (Compl. 19, 20.) "[A]t the time of the transactions at issue herein, Nancy Becerra was employed by [BANA/BAC] as a managing agent." (Id. 8.) Defendants also actually knew of scams such as the "tech support scam" because they "were on notice to look out for, elderly people being swindled by increasingly common scams, including 'tech support scams' prior to the suspicious wire transfers made by Plaintiff at [BANA/BAC] branches as described herein." (Id. 27.)
Defendants, including BANA/BAC managing agent Nancy Becerra, "ignored" all of the information it knew regarding Plaintiff, his prior activity as their longtime customer, and about financial elder abuse scams exactly like this one, "and instead, substantially assisted the scammers in taking Plaintiff's life savings." (Id. 8, 31.) Plaintiff alleges sufficient ultimate facts to suggest that Defendants provided banking services to Plaintiff, knew that severe financial injury could result from Defendant's conduct, and continued to provide services regardless. These allegations are sufficient, at the pleadings stage, to support a claim for punitive damages.

Order is signed.
